title: A Complete Unknown
logline: A young Bob Dylan arrives in New York City in 1961, seeking his musical hero Woody Guthrie, and transforms from folk traditionalist to electric rock pioneer, culminating in his controversial performance at the 1965 Newport Folk Festival.
antagonistOrConflict: The folk music establishment's resistance to change and Bob's internal struggle between artistic authenticity and commercial/artistic evolution
tone: Intimate, rebellious, and melancholic with moments of triumph and controversy
themes: The tension between tradition and innovation
The power of music to reflect social change

actBreaks.description: Bob's arrival in NYC and discovery of the folk scene

validation.ruling: Scene #1 functions as Act 1 end - Bob's arrival in NYC establishes the story world and his initial circumstances as he begins his folk journey.

actBreaks.description: Bob's rise to fame and relationship with Joan Baez

validation.ruling: Scene #42 marks a major shift - Bob watches Kennedy during the Cuban Missile Crisis, representing the moment when external political events begin influencing his artistic evolution toward more complex, non-traditional folk music.

actBreaks.description: Bob's electric transformation and Newport controversy

validation.ruling: Scene #85 is the definitive Act 3 break - Bob recording 'Subterranean Homesick Blues' with a full band represents his complete departure from solo folk, shocking Hammond and Grossman. This fundamental transformation launches the final act's conflict.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality: Classic three-act biographical structure following Bob's journey from unknown folkie to controversial electric pioneer. The story moves chronologically through key relationships and musical evolution, building toward the climactic Newport Folk Festival performance.
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: Short, punchy scenes (1.4 pages average) create energetic momentum. The pacing accelerates in Act 3 as tensions mount toward Newport. Musical performances are interwoven throughout to show artistic development.

incitingIncident.description: Bob's pivotal first meeting with his idol Woody Guthrie, who lies sick in bed while Pete Seeger plays beside him. Bob performs a song for Woody, establishing the connection that brought him to New York.
incitingIncident.sceneHeading: INT. WOODY’S ROOM -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- CONTINUOUS

validation.ruling: Scene #10 is the perfect inciting incident - Bob's first meeting with his idol Woody Guthrie, facilitated by Pete Seeger, launches his entire journey into the folk scene. This moment at 5.6% establishes the central relationships and sets Bob's story in motion.

midpoint.description: Pete Seeger visits dying Woody Guthrie in the hospital, discussing Bob's gift of a record player and reflecting on their own careers and future plans.
midpoint.sceneHeading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- WOODY'S ROOM -- NIGHT

validation.ruling: Scene #65 serves as an effective midpoint at 44% - Pete acknowledges Bob's success ('Bobby's a big part of that') while planning his own world tour. This represents the peak of Bob's folk acceptance before his electric transformation begins.

climax.description: Pete Seeger performs with six actual prisoners and an armed guard. Alan Lomax tells Pete they couldn't convince Bob and that Pete must be the one to handle the situation.
climax.sceneHeading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- STAGE -- NIGHT

validation.ruling: Scene #111 at 82.4% is the climactic moment - Pete on stage while Lomax desperately seeks him to convince Bob not to go electric. This is the final attempt by the folk establishment to stop Bob's transformation, representing the story's ultimate confrontation.

scenes.heading: INT. STATION WAGON -- WET DAY -- WINTER 1961
scenes.summary: Winter 1961: Young Bob Dylan (19) rides in a station wagon through New Jersey marshlands toward New York City, clutching a notebook with songs including 'Song for Woody' as the Empire State Building comes into view.

scenes.heading: EXT. 10TH AVENUE -- WET DAY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Bob is dropped off at the Lincoln Tunnel exit in Hell's Kitchen with his guitar and bag, alone in the harsh gray cityscape as he checks a clipping and heads south.

scenes.heading: INT. FEDERAL COURTROOM -- SOUTHERN DISTRICT -- WET DAY
scenes.summary: In federal court, folk legend Pete Seeger faces sentencing before a judge, defending himself against charges related to his associations while supporters fill the courtroom.

scenes.heading: EXT. BLEEKER AND MACDOUGAL -- SAME WET AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Bob arrives in Greenwich Village's MacDougal Street, encountering the vibrant folk scene with its coffee shops, clubs, and bohemian atmosphere as he searches for his destination.

scenes.heading: EXT. 110 MACDOUGAL STREET -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob discovers the Folk-Lore Center record shop, seeing albums by his heroes including Woody Guthrie, but finds it closed with a sign directing people to the courthouse.

scenes.heading: INT. THE KETTLE OF FISH BAR -- SAME
scenes.summary: In the nearly empty Kettle of Fish bar, Bob asks for directions to Greystone Hospital where Woody Guthrie is confined, learning from musician Dave Van Ronk that it's a mental institution.

scenes.heading: EXT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE -- LATE WET AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Outside the federal courthouse, Pete Seeger performs Woody Guthrie's 'This Land Was Made for You and Me' for supporters and press after his sentencing, with protesters nearby.

scenes.heading: EXT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- NEW JERSEY -- WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob arrives at the gothic Greystone Hospital in New Jersey at night, paying his last two dollars to a cab driver who abandons him at the institution.

scenes.heading: INT. HALLWAY -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob walks through hospital corridors toward a lit room where he hears Pete Seeger's voice singing, approaching the room where his hero Woody Guthrie lies ill.

scenes.heading: INT. WOODY’S ROOM -- GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Bob's pivotal first meeting with his idol Woody Guthrie, who lies sick in bed while Pete Seeger plays beside him. Bob performs a song for Woody, establishing the connection that brought him to New York.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob meets his idol Woody Guthrie, the moment that validates his journey to New York and establishes his place in the folk tradition

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. PETE SEEGER’S STATION WAGON -- SAME WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Driving through the night, Pete and Bob bond over music tastes including Little Richard and Johnny Cash, revealing Bob's eclectic influences beyond traditional folk music.

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. WASHINGTON HEIGHTS -- SAME
scenes.summary: Pete offers Bob shelter at his Hudson Valley home when Bob has no confirmed place to stay downtown, showing Pete's generosity to the young musician.

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER CABIN -- HUDSON VALLEY -- SAME WET NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob arrives at Pete and Toshi Seeger's rustic log cabin home, marveling at their self-built house as they welcome him for the night.

scenes.heading: INT. PETE SEEGER’S HOUSE -- SAME NIGHT
scenes.summary: The Seeger children watch the mysterious young stranger as Pete and Toshi discuss Pete's prison sentence and upcoming tour plans, while Bob smokes outside.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- PETE & TOSHI’S BEDROOM -- LATER
scenes.summary: Morning at the Seegers': Bob composes and plays his song about the north country fair while the children watch him, establishing his songwriter identity.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- LIVING ROOM -- MORNING
scenes.summary: A month later, Bob watches from backstage as Pete Seeger commands the Town Hall audience, demonstrating the power and reach of established folk performers.

scenes.heading: INT. TOWN HALL CONCERT HALL -- A MONTH LATER
scenes.summary: Young folk sensation Joan Baez arrives at Gerdes Folk City to excited fans, while talent manager Albert Grossman waits in her dressing room with rave reviews.

scenes.heading: EXT. MERCER STREET -- EARLY EVENING
scenes.summary: Joan Baez performs 'House of the Rising Sun' at Gerdes Folk City to a packed, enthusiastic crowd including industry figures and Bob, who stares at her intently.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob first sees Joan Baez perform, beginning their complex artistic and personal relationship

scenes.heading: INT. THE GERDES FOLK CITY -- DRESSING ROOM -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob busks in Washington Square Park with a partner, playing children's songs for donations before collecting money and rushing off in a taxi.

scenes.heading: INT. GERDES FOLK CITY -- LATER
scenes.summary: Albert Grossman angrily confronts Bob outside Columbia Records for being late to his recording session, revealing he carries a gun and the pressure of Bob's record deal.

scenes.heading: EXT. WEST VILLAGE PARK -- DAY
scenes.summary: In the Columbia Records elevator, Albert reads Bob a rave review from the New York Times describing him as a cross between a choir boy and beatnik bursting with talent.

scenes.heading: EXT. COLUMBIA RECORDS -- MIDTOWN -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob records his debut album at Columbia Records studio, working with producer John Hammond while struggling with microphone technique during his performance of traditional songs.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA RECORDS BUILDING -- ELEVATOR -- SAME
scenes.summary: At Riverside Church, Bob opens for blues legends Sonny Terry & Brownie McGhee, performing original material while being recorded by documentarians including a young woman named Gena.

scenes.heading: INT. RECORDING STUDIO -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob walks with Sylvie after his performance, philosophizing about carnival freaks and stage performers, suggesting that anyone who holds attention must be somewhat of a freak.

scenes.heading: INT. RIVERSIDE CHURCH -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie watch a classic Hollywood movie together, seeing Paul Henreid and Bette Davis in a romantic scene from a 1940s film.

scenes.heading: EXT. RIVERSIDE CHURCH -- SAME
scenes.summary: Over dinner in Chinatown, Bob and Sylvie discuss the movie's themes of self-transformation, with Bob arguing that people don't 'find themselves' but make themselves into what they want to be.

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE AND BOB IN THE MOVIE THEATER -- SAME
scenes.summary: Walking through the Village at night, Sylvie describes her activism with CORE (Congress of Racial Equality) organizing freedom rides, as they reach her subway stop.

scenes.heading: INT. CHINATOWN RESTAURANT -- LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob visits a record shop and discovers his debut album isn't selling while Joan Baez's records need constant reordering, highlighting the contrast in their commercial success.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB AND SYLVIE WALK THROUGH THE VILLAGE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Watching news coverage of KKK attacks on Freedom Riders buses, Bob is moved to write 'Blowin' in the Wind' on a bar napkin, inspired by the civil rights struggle.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob writes 'Blowin' in the Wind,' his first major protest song that will define his early career

scenes.heading: INT. WEST VILLAGE RECORD SHOP -- NEW DAY
scenes.summary: Bob performs his new song 'Blowin' in the Wind' for Woody Guthrie at Greystone Hospital, with Woody riveted by the powerful new composition before being interrupted by an orderly.

scenes.heading: INT. WEST VILLAGE BAR -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie attend a civil rights rally where speakers discuss the ongoing segregation and violence, questioning whether racists have any conscience to appeal to.

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- WOODY’S ROOM -- DAY
scenes.summary: A mailman delivers a package addressed to 'Zimmerman' (Bob's real name), surprising Sylvie who knows him only as Bob Dylan, hinting at his constructed identity.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB AND SYLVIE AT A CIVIL RIGHTS RALLY -- DAY
scenes.summary: At a party in Bob's apartment, musicians debate musical categories while guests admire Sylvie's paintings and her sister Gena examines Bob's notebooks and records.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- AFTERNOON -- SAME
scenes.summary: After the party, Bob plays guitar along with Leadbelly records while Sylvie tries to sleep, showing his obsessive dedication to music over rest or relationships.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At dawn, Bob continues writing feverishly, composing surreal imagery about newborn babies and highways of diamonds while Sylvie urges him to sleep.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB'S APARTMENT -- NIGHT -- LATE
scenes.summary: By late summer, Sylvie packs for a trip to Italy while Bob watches Johnny Cash on TV and dismisses the host's attempts to categorize music, as their relationship faces separation.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAWN -- NEXT MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger arrives at a small Southern church with civil rights organizers, encountering state police and white protesters with racist signs surrounding the building.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY -- LATE SUMMER
scenes.summary: Inside the Southern church, Pete witnesses powerful spirituals performed by the congregation, feeling the deep connection between music and the civil rights movement.

scenes.heading: EXT. A SMALL SOUTHERN CHURCH -- DAY
scenes.summary: Joan Baez gives a careful interview about preserving traditional songs, while Bob watches President Kennedy address the Cuban Missile Crisis on television, sensing global tension.

scenes.heading: INT. ALBANY, GEORGIA -- SMALL SOUTHERN CHURCH -- DAY
scenes.summary: As the Cuban Missile Crisis unfolds, Pete and Toshi return home to find their babysitter fleeing and their children frightened about the possibility of war.

scenes.heading: INT. VILLAGE COFFEE HOUSE -- DAY
scenes.summary: During the nuclear crisis, Pete criticizes Kennedy while watching the news with his family, and Joan sits alone in her hotel room unable to reach anyone by phone.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: At a nearly empty diner, Bob encounters people preparing for nuclear war, with a fallout shelter in the basement and customers glued to crisis coverage on television.

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER HOUSE -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Joan walks through eerily empty Village streets during the crisis, passing panicked families, until she hears Bob's voice and finds the Gaslight club.

scenes.heading: INT. SEEGER HOUSE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At the Gaslight during the crisis, Bob performs powerful protest songs to a packed room of people with nowhere else to go, including Joan who has just arrived.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob and Joan meet during the Cuban Missile Crisis, bonding during humanity's darkest moment

scenes.heading: INT. CHELSEA HOTEL ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob and Joan wake together as the missile crisis ends, with the world returning to normal. They talk about Bob's musical influences and his mysterious carnival background, deepening their connection.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob and Joan's intimate morning after marks the beginning of their romantic and professional partnership

scenes.heading: INT./EXT. DINER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At the Seeger cabin the morning after the crisis, Pete sits peacefully playing his banjo and singing about conformity while Toshi makes breakfast for their children inside.

scenes.heading: EXT. VILLAGE STREET -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. THE GASLIGHT -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAWN
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: EXT. SEEGER CABIN -- MORNING
scenes.summary: (no summary)

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. BOB’S APARTMENT / HALLWAY -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob frantically cleans his apartment as Sylvie arrives unexpectedly with luggage, rushing to hide evidence of another woman before embracing her at the door.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA RECORDS STUDIO “A” -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob records in Columbia Studio A with new producer Tom Wilson and Albert Grossman observing, performing his original composition.

scenes.heading: EXT. JONES & 4TH STREET -- SNOW -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie playfully pose in the snow for a photographer as Bob continues singing, creating the iconic album cover image.

scenes.heading: EXT. SAN FRANCISCO CONCERT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Joan Baez performs Bob's song at an outdoor San Francisco concert while being recorded by a film crew.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Albert supervises a photo shoot in Bob's apartment while Bob packs for a trip, wrapping a turntable as a gift for Woody Guthrie.

scenes.heading: EXT. HIGHWAY 1 -- NORTHERN CALIFORNIA
scenes.summary: Bob rides his motorcycle up the California coast highway to Joan Baez's house in Carmel Highlands.

scenes.heading: INT. JOAN’S HOUSE -- CARMEL HIGHLANDS, CALIFORNIA
scenes.summary: Joan shows Bob around her beautiful coastal home while his new record plays, with subtle tension over a guitar that buzzes when Bob plays it.

scenes.heading: EXT. MONTEREY FESTIVAL -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the Monterey Festival, Bob receives a lukewarm reception from the crowd as the host introduces him mainly through other artists' versions of his songs.

scenes.heading: EXT. ONSTAGE AT MONTEREY -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Joan joins Bob on stage at Monterey when he struggles with the crowd's tepid response, instantly energizing the audience with her presence.
scenes.turningPointNote: Joan's intervention saves Bob's performance and demonstrates her star power versus his emerging fame

scenes.heading: EXT. EL PASO -- CLUB PARKING LOT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Johnny Cash emerges from a Texas bar and is drawn to Bob's music playing on a car radio, climbing into strangers' vehicle to listen.

scenes.heading: INT. LOBBY -- CBS / COLUMBIA RECORDS -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob picks up fan mail at Columbia Records, receiving a large sack of letters and seeing the album cover featuring him and Sylvie prominently displayed.

scenes.heading: EXT. 52ND STREET / CBS RECORDS -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob walks away from CBS with fan mail and two special letters, including a substantial check and a letter from Johnny Cash with a playful return address.

scenes.heading: EXT. PARK -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob sits in a park reading Johnny Cash's deeply personal letter praising his 'Freewheelin'' album as Cash's most prized possession.

scenes.heading: INT. NEW YORK CITY -- TOWN HALL THEATER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob performs his first major solo concert at Town Hall to devoted fans, while secretly watching people buy his records incognito at a record shop.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's transition from folk circles to having his own dedicated fanbase, marking his emergence as a star

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- WOODY'S ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger visits dying Woody Guthrie in the hospital, discussing Bob's gift of a record player and reflecting on their own careers and future plans.
scenes.turningPointNote: Woody's declining health represents the passing of the folk torch to the new generation

scenes.heading: EXT. DC HOTEL -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: Bob emerges from a hotel to screaming fans who grab and kiss him, forcing him to fight his way to a waiting car.

scenes.heading: INT. EASTERN AIRLINES SHUTTLE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: On an airplane, Bob writes a letter to Johnny Cash about the overwhelming burden of sudden fame while fellow passengers stare and whisper about him.

scenes.heading: INT. NORTH CENTRAL JETLINER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Johnny Cash reads Bob's letter on a turbulent flight and writes a reply on an airsickness bag, promising to visit Bob's hometown of Duluth.

scenes.heading: EXT. CAFE -- MACDOUGAL STREET -- WINTER -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob reads Johnny's humorous letter at a cafe but is recognized by fans who press against the window, forcing him to flee.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie watch Walter Cronkite announce President Kennedy's assassination on television, both visibly shaken by the news.
scenes.turningPointNote: JFK's assassination marks a cultural turning point that will influence Bob's artistic evolution

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- 1964 -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the 1964 Newport Folk Festival, Joan performs while the crowd chants for Bob, who watches both the audience and Sylvie as Johnny Cash arrives backstage.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- 1964 -- LATER
scenes.summary: Johnny Cash introduces Bob at Newport with poetic praise about Bob's mind being like marked paper, leading to Bob's triumphant performance.

scenes.heading: INT. THE KETTLE OF FISH -- SIX MONTHS LATER -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Six months later at the Kettle of Fish, a transformed Bob with wild hair and dark glasses sits detached from a gathering of folk musicians clearly trying to get his attention.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's physical and psychological transformation signals his growing alienation from the folk scene

scenes.heading: EXT. MACDOUGAL STREET -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob walks through a changed Greenwich Village with more tourists and commercial music, drawing stares as he heads to his motorcycle.

scenes.heading: EXT. BOB CRUISES ON THE TRIUMPH -- NIGHT -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob rides his Triumph motorcycle through traffic without a helmet, confirming his identity to passengers in another car before speeding away.

scenes.heading: INT. HAROLD LEVENTHAL’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At Harold Leventhal's civil rights fundraiser, Bob and his new girlfriend Becka navigate a crowd of intellectuals debating whether songs can change the world.

scenes.heading: INT. LEVENTHAL APARTMENT -- MINUTES LATER
scenes.summary: Bob reluctantly performs at the fundraiser, uncomfortable with Pete Seeger's introduction praising him as a symbol for all of folk music.

scenes.heading: INT. LEVENTHAL APARTMENT -- LATER
scenes.summary: Bob and Becka exit through a gauntlet of glad-handers making requests and commitments while Albert manages the social obligations.

scenes.heading: INT. ELEVATOR -- SAME
scenes.summary: In the elevator, Bob complains about people wanting him to be someone else, leading to an encounter with drunk musician Bobby Neuwirth who challenges him.

scenes.heading: EXT. WALKING ON THE STREET -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Bob pursues friendship with Neuwirth while Becka struggles to keep up, culminating in her questioning whether she's just 'more weight' to him.

scenes.heading: EXT. EAST VILLAGE STREETS -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob stands outside McAnn's bar hearing the thumping bass music that draws him to Neuwirth's energetic Celtic band performance.

scenes.heading: INT. MCANN’S -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob watches Neuwirth's raucous band with fascination until a drunk woman recognizes him, causing chaos as he tries to escape losing his sunglasses.

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: At 4 AM, a bloodied Bob shows up at Sylvie's new apartment where she lives with another man, seeking comfort while questioning the source of his songs.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's crisis moment where he questions his artistic source while facing personal isolation

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S APARTMENT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Alone in his apartment with Sylvie's presence erased, Bob works on song fragments when he hears distant electric music that draws him to his radio.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob records 'Subterranean Homesick Blues' with a full electric band, shocking Hammond, Albert, and Tom Wilson in the control room with his new direction.
scenes.turningPointNote: Bob's first full electric recording session represents his artistic transformation and break from folk tradition

scenes.heading: EXT. CHELSEA HOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Neuwirth arrives at Columbia Studios with both acoustic and electric guitar cases, the electric bearing a British Airways sticker from London.

scenes.heading: INT. HALLWAY -- CHELSEA HOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob seeks out Joan at the Chelsea Hotel after seeing Pete's TV show, finding her with marijuana as they reconnect.

scenes.heading: INT. CHELSEA HOTEL ROOM -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: In Joan's hotel room, Bob obsessively writes lyrics while high, but Joan interrupts his creative flow, leading to conflict about why he came to see her.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob records with his full electric band using a toy siren, creating 'Highway 61 Revisited' while Tom Wilson, Albert, and Neuwirth watch from the booth.

scenes.heading: INT. LOMAX OFFICE -- WEST 46TH -- DAY
scenes.summary: The Newport Folk Foundation board meets at Alan Lomax's office to plan the 1965 festival, debating electric acts and Bob's new direction.

scenes.heading: INT. LOMAX OFFICE -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: The folk establishment continues their meeting, with Pete Seeger expressing concern about Bob 'going electric' and its impact on the festival's integrity.

scenes.heading: EXT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- JUNE 1965 -- DAY
scenes.summary: Neuwirth arrives at Columbia Studios haggard from travel, retrieving Bob's new red Fender Stratocaster from a taxi trunk with British Airways sticker.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: In the empty studio, Neuwirth sets up Bob's gleaming red Fender electric guitar while Tom Wilson reacts to the news that Bob bought it in London.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- CONTROL BOOTH -- LATER
scenes.summary: Al Kooper reluctantly agrees to play organ despite not being a keyboardist, while Tom Wilson focuses on Mike Bloomfield's electric guitar work.

scenes.heading: INT. STUDIO A -- JUST AS TAPE’S ABOUT TO ROLL
scenes.summary: Recording 'Like a Rolling Stone,' Bob directs the band to abandon waltz time while Al Kooper struggles with the organ Tom has to turn on for him.

scenes.heading: INT. COLUMBIA STUDIO A -- CONTROL BOOTH -- LATER
scenes.summary: During playback, Bob insists on raising Al Kooper's organ in the mix despite Tom Wilson's protests that he's not really an organ player.

scenes.heading: INT. PAUL SARGENT CLOTHING STORE -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob buys spectacular polka dot shirts for Newport while Pete Seeger spots their motorcycles outside and peers through the store window.

scenes.heading: EXT. PAUL SARGENT STORE -- 4TH STREET -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob encounters Pete outside the clothing store, with awkward tension as Pete questions him about going electric while fans interrupt their conversation.
scenes.turningPointNote: The confrontation between Bob and Pete represents the generational and artistic divide about to explode at Newport

scenes.heading: INT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Bob calls up to Sylvie from the street inviting her to Newport, but she angrily slams her window shut in response.

scenes.heading: EXT. SYLVIE’S APARTMENT -- MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Despite her anger, Sylvie emerges with a backpack and joins Bob on his motorcycle for the ride to Newport, knowing it's probably a mistake.

scenes.heading: EXT. ON THE ROAD TO NEWPORT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie ride the Triumph motorcycle toward Newport with Manhattan behind them. Bob's 'Mr. Tambourine Man' plays on radios in multiple cars, with fans singing along, showing his growing popularity.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE STREETS OF NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Bob and Sylvie ride through Newport's main street where he's recognized by townspeople who point, nod, and call his name. He grins but keeps driving as fans continue singing his song.

scenes.heading: EXT. VIKING MOTEL -- NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: At the Viking Motel, Bob and Sylvie encounter a diverse gathering of international folk musicians. Jesse Moffette greets Bob, noting he looks different and asking about Sylvie, who responds she's 'only wild on weekends.'

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S ROOM, THE VIKING MOTEL -- DAY
scenes.summary: In their motel room, Bob recreates a classic movie scene by lighting two cigarettes for himself and Sylvie. Neuwirth bursts in with guitar cases containing Bob's acoustic Gibson and a red Stratocaster from London.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- DAY
scenes.summary: Joan performs on the Newport Folk Festival stage to a huge crowd. Backstage, Bob arrives with Sylvie and Neuwirth, encounters Pete, and Joan discretely gives Bob the finger when she sees him with his guitar.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Joan improvises by singing one of Bob's songs with flipped gender pronouns. Bob laughs and joins her for the second verse, showing their musical chemistry despite personal tensions.

scenes.heading: INT. DINER -- DOWNTOWN NEWPORT -- DAY
scenes.summary: Alan Lomax and Pete discuss Bob's intentions over lunch. Lomax expresses concern about Top 40 radio's influence and insists they must stand against it, while Pete says Bob will do what he wants.

scenes.heading: INT. / EXT. VIKING HOTEL -- BOB’S SUITE -- LATE AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: At a crowded party in Bob's motel suite, folk establishment figures visit to confront Bob about his plans. The atmosphere is tense with smoke, people, and underlying conflict about musical direction.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT STREETS -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: Bob races through Newport streets on his motorcycle, ignoring fans who wave and shout to him. He appears to be fleeing from something or someone.

scenes.heading: EXT. PROVIDENCE FERRY TERMINAL -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: At Providence Ferry Terminal, Sylvie is leaving when Bob arrives on his Triumph. They meet at a fence where he lights cigarettes for both, but she flicks hers away and tells him to quit smoking.
scenes.turningPointNote: Sylvie appears to be ending her relationship with Bob

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FOLK FESTIVAL -- STAGE -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete Seeger performs with six actual prisoners and an armed guard. Alan Lomax tells Pete they couldn't convince Bob and that Pete must be the one to handle the situation.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT -- VIKING PARKING LOT -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete sits in his pickup truck across from the Viking Motel parking lot, watching as Bloomfield and Al Kooper leave Bob's suite. Bob appears briefly shirtless before the door closes.

scenes.heading: INT. BOB’S SUITE. VIKING MOTEL -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Pete enters Bob's trashed suite with coffee, finding bodies and chaos. Bob is in bed with Albert Grossman instead of Sylvie. Pete notices the red Stratocaster and tries to convince Bob about his performance choice.
scenes.turningPointNote: Key confrontation between Pete and Bob about going electric

scenes.heading: EXT. VIKING MOTEL -- MORNING
scenes.summary: Bob encounters Johnny Cash at the motel vending machine. Cash appears exhausted or high and doesn't initially recognize Bob. They have a brief conversation about Cash staying for Bob's finale performance.

scenes.heading: INT. VIKING HOTEL, NEWPORT -- BOB’S SUITE -- LATE DAY
scenes.summary: In his suite late in the day, Bob washes his face and sees a reflection of himself from four years ago outside Guthrie's hospital room. Neuwirth enters, noting it's getting crazy outside.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING MOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Bob heads to his controversial electric performance at Newport Folk Festival. Backstage, all eyes are on him as the folk establishment watches tensely. The stage is set with electric equipment as anticipation builds.
scenes.turningPointNote: The climactic electric performance at Newport - the central controversy of the film

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. CAR IN LOADING AREA -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Neuwirth quickly leave in a car as the festival finale continues in the background, suggesting a hasty exit after the controversial performance.

scenes.heading: INT/EXT. CAR -- OVER BOB AND NEUWIRTH -- SAME
scenes.summary: Bob and Neuwirth arrive back at the Viking Motel where a party is spilling out onto the balcony, indicating the aftermath of the electric performance.

scenes.heading: INT. CROWDED ROOM AT THE VIKING -- LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: At the Viking party, Albert reads Bob a positive review from The Village Voice praising his performance as groundbreaking rather than betrayal, while Bob sits observing the celebration around him.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING MOTEL -- NIGHT
scenes.summary: Pete and Toshi return to their motel room post-show. Pete considers joining the party where Bob is but ultimately turns away after seeing Bob alone across the crowded room.

scenes.heading: INT. PARTY ROOM AT THE VIKING -- DAWN
scenes.summary: At dawn, stragglers remain at the party while Bob sits alone in his chair. He gets up and heads for the door, still isolated despite the celebration around him.

scenes.heading: EXT. THE VIKING HOTEL PARKING LOT -- DAWN
scenes.summary: Joan confronts Bob at his motorcycle, telling him he won his freedom. Bob questions what he's won, Joan acknowledges he was hurt by being called Judas. They part with mutual respect and raised hands.
scenes.turningPointNote: Resolution between Bob and Joan about the cost of his artistic choice

scenes.heading: EXT. NEWPORT FESTIVAL GROUNDS -- DAWN
scenes.summary: Bob encounters Pete cleaning up the festival grounds. Pete sings lyrics from Bob's 'Desolation Row' and admits he listened to Bob's album after all, showing grudging respect for Bob's artistry.
scenes.turningPointNote: Pete's acknowledgment and acceptance of Bob's artistic evolution

scenes.heading: INT. GREYSTONE HOSPITAL -- DAWN
scenes.summary: At dawn in Greystone Hospital, Bob sits with the dying Woody Guthrie playing harmonica to a Woody Guthrie record. The scene connects Bob's journey back to his original inspiration and mentor.
scenes.turningPointNote: Emotional climax - Bob returns to his roots and dying mentor Woody Guthrie
